did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning and metaheuristic algorithms for melanoma detection. Specifically, it extracted handcrafted features from MED-NODE dataset images and used Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), and Simulated Annealing (SA) for feature selection. Classification was performed using Support Vector Machine (SVM) and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifiers, achieving 87.69% accuracy with PSO-selected features and ANN.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the extraction of 1,898 handcrafted features from the MED-NODE dataset, optimization using metaheuristic algorithms (PSO, ACO, SA), and classification using SVM and ANN. It reports specific new results, including a best accuracy of 87.69%, and compares these findings with prior literature, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Melanoma Detection From Lesion Images Using Optimized Features Selected by Metaheuristic Algorithms
journal: International Journal of Healthcare Information Systems and Informatics
brief_summary: This study addresses the detection of malignant melanoma from non-dermoscopy lesion images using optimized handcrafted features. The authors extracted 1,898 shape, color, and texture features from 170 images in the MED-NODE dataset and applied three metaheuristic algorithms—Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), and Simulated Annealing (SA)—for feature selection. Classification was performed using Support Vector Machine (SVM) and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifiers. The method achieved a best classification accuracy of 87.69% using only seven features selected by PSO with the ANN classifier. This result demonstrates that high accuracy can be achieved with a significantly reduced feature set compared to prior literature.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task. The MED-NODE dataset contains 100 images of malignant melanoma, verified by biopsy.
One of the two classes in the binary classification task. The MED-NODE dataset contains 70 images of benign nevi, confirmed by expert dermato-pathologist.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into malignant melanoma or benign nevi
all_task_types.details: The paper performs a two-class binary classification task to distinguish between malignant melanoma and benign nevi using the MED-NODE dataset. Supervised classifiers (SVM and ANN) are used to predict discrete diagnostic categories based on optimized handcrafted features.

datasets_used.name: MED-NODE
datasets_used.description: A publicly available standard melanoma dataset consisting of non-dermoscopy camera images (.jpeg format) archived in the Dermatology department, Groningen University. Images were taken by a Nikon camera with a 2.8/105 mm lens at an average distance of 330 mm. Ground truth for malignant melanoma was verified by biopsy, and benign nevi were confirmed by an expert dermato-pathologist.

use.training: Used for feature extraction and classification via SVM and ANN classifiers with metaheuristic feature selection (PSO, ACO, SA). The paper does not report a specific train/validation/test split, implying the entire dataset was used for the experimental pipeline.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Results are presented as classification accuracy percentages derived from 5-fold cross-validation, with comparisons made to prior literature based on accuracy values rather than statistical significance.

MED-NODE.notes: The proposed work achieved the best accuracy of 87.69% using 7 features selected by PSO with an ANN classifier. This result is compared against previous works on the same MED-NODE dataset. The paper notes that higher accuracy was achieved with less than 0.4% of all features selected by the optimizations. Table 5 lists comparison results from related works.

architecture_used.main_model: Support Vector Machine (SVM) and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) / Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)

ANN/MLP.hidden_layers: Variable (optimized by varying neurons from 1 to half the number of features)

training_details.feature_selection: Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), Simulated Annealing (SA)
training_details.hardware: Intel Core i5-6500, 3.2 GHz processor with 8 GB-RAM

classification_or_output_head.type: Binary classification (Malignant melanoma vs. Benign nevi)
classification_or_output_head.best_configuration: 7 features selected by PSO with ANN classifier

conclusions: The Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) feature selection algorithm performed significantly better than Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) and Simulated Annealing (SA) for selecting optimized handcrafted features from melanoma lesion images.
The proposed method achieved a best classification accuracy of 87.69% using only seven features selected by PSO combined with an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifier.
High classification accuracy can be achieved with a drastically reduced feature set (less than 0.4% of the original 1,898 features), demonstrating the efficiency of metaheuristic feature selection.

Texture features were identified as the most effective feature category for skin lesion classification, with the best-performing feature set consisting of one first-order color feature and six second-order texture features (GLRLM and GLCM).
The ANN classifier outperformed the Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier across most feature sets tested in this study.

The proposed method's accuracy (87.69%) is comparable to the performance of 157 dermatologists (89.4% sensitivity, 64.4% specificity) on the same MED-NODE dataset, suggesting that computer-aided detection can match expert human performance.

The study demonstrates that non-dermoscopy camera images can be effectively used for melanoma detection using optimized handcrafted features, offering a less costly alternative to biopsy or expensive deep learning models.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, single-center dataset (MED-NODE, n=170) with no external validation, limiting generalizability.
The paper does not report a specific train/validation/test split, implying potential data leakage or overfitting risks as the entire dataset appears used for the experimental pipeline.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; comparisons with prior literature are based solely on accuracy percentages.

The best accuracy (87.69%) is lower than a previous work by the same authors (Mukherjee et al., 2018b) which reported 91.02% accuracy, though the current method uses significantly fewer features (7 vs 1886).
The dataset consists of non-dermoscopy images, which may not reflect the performance on standard dermoscopy images used in clinical practice.

The comparison with dermatologist performance (Brinker et al., 2019) is indirect, as the dermatologists' results were from a separate survey using the same dataset, not a direct head-to-head comparison in this study.
